title: Periodic automated cleanup of completed tasks and ideas
summary: Completed and cancelled entries don't need to live in the active store indefinitely — everything is in git history. A periodic cleanup (e.g. daily, weekly, or N days after marking done) deletes done/cancelled entries from the JSONL and .md files, keeping the active store lean. If historical data is ever needed, a domus task history command could retrieve it from git. Key open questions: what trigger (time-based vs manual vs on-exit), what threshold (immediate, N days, never for certain tags), and whether to build the git-retrieval path before or after enabling cleanup.

title: Domus Skills Plugin System
summary: Package Claude skills (capture-task, capture-idea, etc.) inside the domus repo and install them via a domus command, so skills are versioned alongside the CLI they wrap.

title: Subagent Strategy for Domus Workflow
summary: Define where Claude subagents add value in the domus/Oracle system without polluting the main conversation context. Which steps (refinement, scoping, task dispatch) benefit from delegation?

title: Domus as the sole gateway to index and task/idea data
summary: Ensure index files (tasks.jsonl, ideas.jsonl) are never loaded into Claude's context directly. Domus CLI should provide all access. Individual md files should only load when text edits are needed, and even then prefer subagents.

title: Testing strategy for domus
summary: How to test the domus CLI and workflow system. Unit tests are straightforward but not yet implemented. E2E testing is unclear — use an existing harness or write a custom one? Some functionality may also need deeper integration-level analysis.

title: Session orientation - how does a new session know what to do next
summary: A new Claude session can run domus task ready to find actionable tasks, but lacks context about priority, recent decisions, and the broader focus area. Need a session-start ritual and/or memory strategy so a fresh session can orient itself without a lengthy briefing.

title: Spec system - behavioural contracts for the codebase
summary: Specs describe what the system should do (intent), while code describes what it actually does (reality). A two-folder model (future/ and current/) lets domus promote spec changes atomically on task completion. A self-healing doctor system catches drift. Spec granularity is the open question.

title: Plans and autonomous tasks are the same artifact
summary: A plan detailed enough for Claude to execute and an autonomous-refinement task contain identical information in different templates. The capture-task skill should produce autonomous-quality tasks directly from a sufficiently detailed scoping conversation, skipping the plan artifact.

title: Global vs project-local domus workspace
summary: Allow domus to operate against a global workspace separate from the current project directory, so .domus/ data isn't polluted into unrelated repos.
outcome_note: Tried building this before and it didn't work. Deferring until there's a concrete use case that makes the right resolution strategy obvious.

title: Domus codebase architecture document
summary: A lightweight ARCHITECTURE.md committed to the domus repo that maps what each file/module does and why, so future sessions can orient quickly without reading all source files.

title: Oracle session UX: pick-then-launch vs launch-then-pick
summary: Unresolved UX question about how the idea refinement session is initiated. Option A: user picks idea first, then Oracle session launches with that idea as context. Option B: Oracle session launches first, lists ideas, user picks from within the session.

title: ADR support in the spec system
summary: Specs describe what the system does (behavioural contracts). ADRs describe why a particular implementation approach was chosen. These belong together — the 'why' informs future refactoring and enhancements by making the original constraints visible. Explore how ADRs sit alongside or within spec files.

title: Interactive session model and launch command design
summary: Current 'work' and 'idea' commands launch separate sessions per persona. Alternative model: one session loads all personas, switch organically via natural language (e.g. 'as the Oracle, let's discuss X'). Either way, commands should follow 'domus launch <persona>' pattern. Must also distinguish interactive (human-present) vs background (worker) launches explicitly — these work very differently and need separate command paths.

title: Standardize idea MD file format to match task MD conventions
summary: Idea MD files use an old ad-hoc format (Date, Project headers) while task MDs have Status, Refinement, Priority, Captured fields kept in sync by the CLI. Should idea MDs adopt the same structure for consistency and doctor-skill cross-checking?

title: Execution state convention in task bodies
summary: Define where and how executing agents write progress back to task bodies. What gets recorded, in what format, at what granularity — so that retry is a true resume and the task list reflects live system state.

title: Surfacing stalled autonomous tasks to the human
summary: How does a stalled or blocked auto task escalate to the human? Should surface as a decision point (here is what I tried, here is where I'm stuck, what should I do) not a technical dump. Human answers, agent continues — not restarts.

title: Auto pipeline observability and control plane
summary: How does the human see what's actively going on with auto tasks? Needs: current state (what's running, what's queued, what's stalled), recent history (what completed, what failed), and controls (re-prioritize, trigger retry, inspect). Execution state written back to task bodies is the data foundation; the question is the interface on top.

title: Context mobility as foundational substrate vs fifth pillar
summary: Context mobility (the ability for any session to pick up any task/idea without explanation) may not be a fifth pillar but the substrate all four pillars rest on. Separately: is 'concept refinement' a true pillar with its own outputs (refined specs, task graphs) or is it what the interactive session layer produces — a pipeline stage rather than a pillar? Both framings have legs. Worth resolving before the pillar model hardens.

title: Human-in-the-loop pre-requisites as a first-class refinement concept
summary: Some tasks can't reach autonomous without a human answering a question first (e.g. 'does the money field support X?'). This should be a first-class concept in the refinement workflow — not just an open question in the task body, but a tracked gate that blocks autonomous promotion until answered. Answering it naturally either closes the gate (task proceeds) or spawns a new blocking task. Domus should make this pattern explicit and easy to act on.

title: Coloured terminal output for domus CLI
summary: Use ANSI colours in domus output to make status, metadata, and structure stand out. e.g. status icons coloured by state (green=done, red=cancelled, yellow=stalled), IDs dimmed, titles prominent. Should respect NO_COLOR env convention. Scope: which commands, which fields, colour scheme TBD.

title: domus init copies skills to ~/.claude/skills/
summary: When setup/skills/ exists in cwd (i.e. you're running init from the domus repo), domus init copies skill directories to ~/.claude/skills/, skipping existing. Makes the global machine setup a single command. Later: --force flag to overwrite, or promote to domus upgrade.

title: Keeping CHANGELOG.md up to date
summary: Explore options for maintaining CHANGELOG.md: Claude updates it as part of task completion, commit hooks (e.g. commitizen, git-cliff), or a domus command that drafts entries from recent commits. Trade-offs: automation vs. curated quality, hook complexity vs. manual discipline.

title: Central execution audit log in JSONL
summary: Alongside per-task execution logs (.domus/logs/<id>.md for resumability), maintain a central .domus/audit.jsonl that appends one entry per significant event: task started, task completed, task stalled, worker dispatched. Provides observability without needing to open individual task files. Queryable and streamable.

title: Track worker branch on task during execution
summary: When a worker picks up a task, record the worktree branch name on the task record (e.g. worker_branch field). Surfaces in domus task show and overview so the human can git log <branch> to monitor progress. Solves the monitoring gap when .domus/ store is split across worktrees — the task record in the main repo is the source of truth for where work is happening.

title: Claude Code usage percentages outside /usage dialog
summary: Expose the 5-hour and weekly usage percentages from Claude Code's /usage dialog via a shell-callable script or domus command, for use in watch loops and monitoring.

title: Observability UX for domus
summary: Design the full observability experience for domus: what does the human see when workers are running, stalled, or done? What's the right surface — task watch, a dedicated pipeline view, terminal output, something else? Inputs we already have: worker branch on task, execution logs, audit log, task status. The 'last 5 done in overview' was one narrow suggestion. The real question is: what does an operator need to feel in control of autonomous work happening on their behalf?

title: improve task overview to give better pipeline progress insight
summary: The current overview is a good first step but lacks nuance — no distinction between actively-in-progress, awaiting-review, and blocked, and the tree structure for blocked tasks raises questions about deeper dependency chains.

title: explicit task ordering so execution sequence is always clear
summary: Tasks need an explicit ordering mechanism so any session can see not just what's open but in what sequence things will be tackled — removing ambiguity about what's next without relying on memory or conversation history.

title: Context alignment checker — keep session context reflecting current reality
summary: As the project evolves, context files (agent-instructions, memory, decisions) can drift out of sync with actual code and design. Need a mechanism (possibly Doctor persona or dedicated persona) to periodically audit context files, identify stale or contradictory content, and either update or archive it. Old context isn't deleted — archived with progressive disclosure so historical info is accessible but not noise in current sessions.

title: Memory portability — move session memory into the repo alongside tasks and decisions
summary: Currently memory lives in ~/.claude/projects/ and doesn't travel with the repo. For a new machine or collaborator to get up to speed, the 'meat' of context should live in .domus/ and be version-controlled like tasks, ideas, and decisions. Explore whether this overlaps with specs or is a distinct concept. The ~/.claude memory mechanism could remain as an auto-loading convenience that points into the repo.

title: Periodic task and idea hygiene review — surface stale backlog items to human
summary: As the project evolves, outstanding tasks and ideas become irrelevant but stay in the backlog as noise. Need a periodic review mechanism (Doctor-type persona) that audits open tasks/ideas against current direction, surfaces inconsistencies and recommendations to the human, who decides: update, cancel, defer, or split. Core philosophy: LLM performance degrades with noisy context — clean, focused context is a first-class concern, not housekeeping.

title: Model tiering for autonomous tasks — haiku or cheaper model for well-refined tasks
summary: If a task is refined to autonomous with enough precision and narrow scope, a smaller/cheaper model (Haiku or equivalent) might be able to execute it reliably. Well-refined tasks with explicit steps, clear acceptance criteria, and tight scope reduce the reasoning burden enough that a capable smaller model could handle it. This could be a meaningful cost saving at scale.

title: Parallelise session recap with specialised subagents
summary: Split the session recap into multiple subagents running in parallel, each owning a different concern — memory, behaviour/feedback, project state, etc. — so recaps are faster and each subagent has a tighter, more focused context.

title: Control worker agent branch names
summary: Worker agents using isolation: worktree get auto-generated branch names. Explore whether we should control naming ourselves for readability.

title: Daemon-style notification system for domus
summary: Herald covers interactive session notifications, but eventually domus may need a background daemon that can surface signals outside of active Claude sessions.

title: Epic with subtasks as standard pattern for dependency tracking
summary: When work is large enough to decompose, create a parent epic that depends-on its subtasks. Subtasks reference the epic for big-picture context. Closing the epic is naturally gated by all subtasks completing.

title: Taskmaster refinement sessions use question-framing for feedback
summary: During refinement review, feedback should be framed as questions to invite the refiner to engage with the reasoning. One-way corrections miss the dialogue that makes refinement useful.

title: Archive folders for completed tasks, ideas, and execution logs
summary: Move done/cancelled task.md files, idea.md files, and execution logs into archive/ subdirectories within their respective folders. Keeps active lists lean as volume grows. Not urgent — git handles history, overview already filters to active. Build when folder size actually causes problems, not before.

title: Explicit domus update command with set-branch
summary: Separate 'domus update' command for re-configuring an existing .domus/ directory (e.g. set branch). Init stays for first-time setup only.

title: Remove DOMUS_ROOT env var — use config.json + --root only
summary: Workers read root from config.json in their worktree copy, then use --root flag. Removes ambient env var that agents could misuse. Simplifies the protocol to a single mechanism.

title: Advisor role — strategic consigliere for backlog and priority guidance
summary: A role that reads the full project state (ideas, deferred tasks, vision docs, backlog shape) and recommends what to refine, undefer, or work on next. Advisory only — human decides.
